title: Choosing a Color Space for a Task
concept: Which color space to use depends on the task's real bottleneck: RGB matches how screens display light, HSV matches how humans describe and isolate color regardless of lighting, and YCbCr matches what compression needs to shrink files without losing much visible quality.

exercises.prompt: Is there one color space that's simply 'the best' for every computer vision task?

options.text: No — the best choice depends on what the task actually needs: display, color-robust detection, or compact storage.
Yes, RGB is always the best choice for everything.
Yes, HSV is always the best choice for everything.

exercises.explain: Right — RGB, HSV, and YCbCr each solve a different problem well.

cards.title: Three tasks, three different tools
cards.body: Showing a photo on a screen, finding a red ball in a video no matter the lighting, and shrinking a video file for streaming are three different jobs. It turns out RGB, HSV, and YCbCr each happen to be the natural fit for one of them.

cards.title: Match the space to the bottleneck
cards.body: Pick a color space by asking what your task is actually limited by. Need to display light on a screen? Use RGB — it's literally how pixels emit light. Need to isolate a color reliably as lighting changes? Use HSV — its Hue channel stays stable while Value shifts. Need to shrink file size for storage or streaming? Use YCbCr — it lets you compress color harder than brightness.

cards.title: Boots, sandals, and dress shoes
cards.body: You don't own one pair of shoes for hiking, the beach, and a wedding — each situation has a shoe that fits it best. RGB, HSV, and YCbCr are the same kind of situational fit: none of them is 'better' in general, each is built for a different job.

cards.title: Three tasks, three picks
cards.body: Building a photo-editing app people will stare at on a screen? RGB — you're directly controlling displayed light. Building a robot that has to grab 'the red block' whether it's near a window or under a lamp? HSV — filter by Hue and shrug off brightness changes. Building a video-call feature that has to work on a slow connection? YCbCr — subsample the chroma channels and cut the data in half.

cards.title: 'Just convert everything to HSV' isn't always right
cards.body: Since HSV sounds more 'intuitive,' it's tempting to assume it should replace RGB everywhere. But a screen doesn't emit Hue and Saturation — it emits red, green, and blue light, so the final step always needs RGB. And HSV doesn't compress efficiently the way YCbCr does. Each color space earns its place by solving one specific problem well, not by being generally superior.

cards.title: Two tasks, two clear winners
cards.body: Different bottlenecks point to different color spaces.

left.tag: Task: track a color under changing light
left.text:
Bottleneck: color must stay recognizable.
RGB triplets shift a lot with brightness.
HSV's Hue barely moves. → Use HSV.

right.tag: Task: shrink a video for streaming
right.text:
Bottleneck: file size / bandwidth.
RGB treats every channel as equally important.
YCbCr lets chroma compress harder. → Use YCbCr.

exercises.prompt: A team is building the final step of an app that draws pixels directly onto a phone screen. Which color space do they need at that final step?

options.text: RGB — screens display light as red, green, and blue intensities.
HSV — screens display hue and saturation directly.
YCbCr — screens display luma and chroma directly.

exercises.explain: Correct — no matter what a pipeline uses internally, the pixels sent to the screen ultimately need to be RGB.

exercises.prompt: True or false: YCbCr is generally a better choice than HSV for compressing a video file efficiently.
exercises.explain: True — YCbCr's brightness/color split is specifically what enables chroma subsampling and efficient compression.

exercises.sentence: The right color space to use depends on the task's ___ — display, color-robust detection, or file size.
exercises.explain: Bottleneck is correct — you pick a color space based on what's actually limiting the task.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'I'll just always use HSV for everything, since it's the most human-friendly color space.' Is that solid reasoning?

options.text: Not quite — HSV is great for color-based detection, but screens still need RGB to display pixels, and YCbCr compresses far better for storage or streaming.
Yes — HSV is strictly better than RGB and YCbCr in every situation.
No — HSV can't represent most real-world colors.

exercises.explain: Right — each color space has a job it does best; 'human-friendly' doesn't make HSV the right choice for display or compression.

exercises.prompt: A team is building a full pipeline: capture video from a camera, detect a specific colored object under changing outdoor light, and stream the result to viewers over a limited connection. Which color-space choices best match each stage?

options.text: RGB from the camera sensor, HSV for the lighting-robust color detection step, and YCbCr for efficient compression before streaming.
HSV for every single stage, since it's the most intuitive color space.
RGB for every single stage, since it's the simplest to think about.
YCbCr for capture and detection, then RGB for streaming to save bandwidth.

exercises.explain: Exactly — RGB matches the sensor/display, HSV matches lighting-robust detection, and YCbCr matches efficient compression: each stage gets the space that fits its bottleneck.
